claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the Child Benefit eligibility rules. To help you determine if you're eligible, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children - Alex, who is 11, and Blake, who is 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex (11 years old) and Blake (15 years old).

Do both Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, actually neither of them live with me. And I don't live in the UK myself - I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand your situation better.

Could you tell me which country you're currently living in?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm currently living in Spain.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Living in Spain may affect your eligibility, as Child Benefit generally requires you to live in the UK (though there are some exceptions for certain countries and circumstances).

Since your children don't live with you, can you tell me - are you contributing financially towards Alex and Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or their accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute a bit towards Alex - I help out with some pocket money and clothes. For Blake, well, their other parent who lives with them is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a follow-up question about your contributions for Alex.

How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep, and how often do you make these contributions? (For reference, the current Child Benefit rate is approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, and £16.95 per week for additional children.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute about £10 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to clarify a few more things to assess your eligibility properly.

You mentioned that Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Is that same person also claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is no one currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No one is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The other parent is only claiming for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Where does Alex currently live? Does Alex live with the other parent in the UK, or somewhere else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with the other parent in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I need to understand a bit more about your work situation to see if any exceptions might apply.

Are you working in Spain? And if so, what type of work are you doing? (For example, are you a Crown servant working for the UK government abroad, or doing regular employment in Spain?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm doing regular employment in Spain - just a normal job there, nothing to do with the UK government.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment.

Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or are you a UK national, or are you an EU/EEA national?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm a UK national.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

Let me explain the outcome for each child:

**For Blake (age 15):**
You are not eligible for Child Benefit because Blake's other parent is already claiming it. Under UK rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for the same child. Since someone else is already receiving Child Benefit for Blake, you cannot also claim it.

**For Alex (age 11):**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex either. Here's why:

1. **Residency requirement**: Child Benefit normally requires you to live in the UK. While you are a UK national, you are living and working in Spain in regular employment (not as a Crown servant), so you don't meet the UK residency requirement.

2. **Contribution amount**: Even if the residency requirement didn't apply, your contributions of £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep are less than the Child Benefit rate for an eldest/only child (approximately £25.60 per week). To qualify when a child lives with someone else, you need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment.

3. **Living arrangement**: Alex lives with the other parent in the UK, and while no one is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, the other parent would likely be the more appropriate claimant as they live with Alex in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry that you're not eligible for Child Benefit in your current circumstances. If your situation changes in the future - for example, if you move back to the UK, or if you increase your contributions to Alex - you may want to contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss your eligibility again.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 11 years old and lives with the other parent in the UK. The claimant lives in Spain in regular employment (not as a Crown servant) and therefore does not meet the UK residency requirement for Child Benefit. Additionally, the claimant contributes only £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is less than the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child. Under the rules, when a child lives with someone else, contributions must be at least equal to the Child Benefit amount to continue receiving it beyond 8 weeks. The claimant does not meet either the residency requirement or the contribution threshold.

Blake is 15 years old and the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for the same child. Since Blake's other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for Blake, the claimant cannot also claim it.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK national living in Spain with two children: Alex (11) and Blake (15). Neither child lives with the claimant; both live with the other parent in the UK. The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. For Blake, the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim for the same child. For Alex, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement as they live and work in Spain in regular employment (not as a Crown servant), and their contributions of £10 per week are below the required threshold to maintain eligibility when a child lives elsewhere.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 11 years old and lives with the other parent in the UK. The claimant lives in Spain in regular employment (not as a Crown servant) and therefore does not meet the UK residency requirement for Child Benefit. Additionally, the claimant contributes only £10 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is less than the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child. Under the rules, when a child lives with someone else, contributions must be at least equal to the Child Benefit amount to continue receiving it beyond 8 weeks. The claimant does not meet either the residency requirement or the contribution threshold.

Blake is 15 years old and the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for the same child. Since Blake's other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for Blake, the claimant cannot also claim it.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK national living in Spain with two children: Alex (11) and Blake (15). Neither child lives with the claimant; both live with the other parent in the UK. The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. For Blake, the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim for the same child. For Alex, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement as they live and work in Spain in regular employment (not as a Crown servant), and their contributions of £10 per week are below the required threshold to maintain eligibility when a child lives elsewhere.
